Gen.1: Six-day creation by divine fiat. God speaks, separates, fills, and pronounces 'good'; humanity is made imago Dei to rule as vice-regents.
Gen.2: Sabbath rest and the Edenic covenant; man formed from dust, woman from his side — marriage instituted as creation ordinance.
Gen.3: The Fall: serpent's deception, federal headship of Adam, curse pronounced, and the protoevangelium (3:15) — first gospel promise.
Gen.4: Cain murders Abel; two lines emerge — the seed of the serpent and the seed of the woman; worship and violence diverge.
Gen.5: Genealogy from Adam to Noah; refrain 'and he died' shows reign of death; Enoch translated, hinting at hope beyond mortality.
Gen.6: Sons of God, daughters of men, and pervasive corruption; God grieves and resolves judgment, but Noah finds grace.
Gen.7: The Flood comes — universal judgment on sin; God shuts Noah in the ark, the only refuge from wrath.
Gen.8: Waters recede; Noah offers sacrifice; God smells the pleasing aroma and pledges cosmic stability.
Gen.9: Noahic covenant: rainbow sign, sanctity of human life, dominion renewed; Noah's drunkenness and Canaan's curse.
Gen.10: Table of Nations — seventy nations descend from Noah's sons, framing the world before Babel.
Gen.11: Babel: human pride scattered by confusion of tongues; genealogy narrows to Abram, the chosen vessel of redemption.
Gen.12: Abrahamic call and covenant: land, seed, blessing — universal scope ('all families blessed') points to Christ.
Gen.13: Abram and Lot separate; Lot chooses sight, Abram trusts promise; God reaffirms the land grant.
Gen.14: Abram rescues Lot; Melchizedek, priest-king of Salem, blesses him — typological foreshadow of Christ's priesthood.
Gen.15: Covenant cut: Abram believes God, counted righteous (sola fide); God alone passes between the pieces.
Gen.16: Hagar and Ishmael — fleshly attempt to fulfill promise; God still sees and provides for the outcast.
Gen.17: Covenant of circumcision; name change to Abraham; sign sealed in flesh, pointing to inward consecration.
Gen.18: Theophany at Mamre; Sarah's laugh; Abraham intercedes for Sodom — God's justice tempered by covenant mercy.
Gen.19: Sodom destroyed; Lot delivered though compromised; warning against worldliness and presumption.
Gen.20: Abraham's deception of Abimelech; God protects the covenant line despite the patriarch's failure.
Gen.21: Isaac born — promise fulfilled; Hagar cast out, Abrahamic covenant narrows to the child of promise.
Gen.22: The Aqedah — Abraham offers Isaac on Moriah; God provides a substitute ram, foreshadowing the gospel and confirming the covenant by oath.
Gen.23: Sarah dies; Abraham buys Machpelah — first land foothold, down-payment on promised inheritance.
Gen.24: Servant seeks Rebekah; providential answer to prayer secures the covenant bride for Isaac.
Gen.25: Abraham dies; Esau and Jacob born — election distinguishes the brothers from the womb (Rom 9).
Gen.26: Isaac reaffirmed in covenant promises; repeats father's deception; wells dug as claims on the land.
Gen.27: Jacob steals Esau's blessing; God's electing purpose stands despite human deceit.
Gen.28: Bethel: ladder vision — heaven opened, God speaks promise to Jacob; Christ later identifies as the Ladder (Jn 1:51).
Gen.29: Jacob deceived by Laban; serves for Leah and Rachel — the deceiver is deceived.
Gen.30: Twelve tribes begin to form through Leah, Rachel, and the maids; Jacob prospers despite Laban's tricks.
Gen.31: Jacob flees Laban; covenant of Mizpah; God's protection vindicates the chosen patriarch.
Gen.32: Wrestling at Peniel; Jacob renamed Israel — sanctification through divine struggle, blessing tied to brokenness.
Gen.33: Reconciliation with Esau; Jacob's gifts received — God turns the heart of the offended brother.
Gen.34: Dinah defiled; Simeon and Levi's vengeance — covenant family tarnished by violence and deceit.
Gen.35: Return to Bethel; vows renewed; Rachel dies bearing Benjamin; tribes complete.
Gen.36: Genealogy of Esau (Edom) — covenant focus narrows again to Jacob's line.
Gen.37: Joseph sold by brothers; dreams of dominion sown amid betrayal — providence working in dark soil.
Gen.38: Judah and Tamar — scandalous insertion preserves Messianic line; grace for the unlikely.
Gen.39: Joseph in Potiphar's house; flees Potiphar's wife; suffering for righteousness, God prospers him.
Gen.40: Joseph interprets dreams in prison — gift of revelation marks him as God's instrument.
Gen.41: Pharaoh's dreams interpreted; Joseph exalted to vizier — humiliation precedes glory, a Christ-pattern.
Gen.42: Brothers come for grain; Joseph tests them; conscience awakened by famine.
Gen.43: Second journey with Benjamin; Judah pledges himself — repentance taking shape.
Gen.44: Cup in Benjamin's sack; Judah's substitutionary plea reveals genuine change of heart.
Gen.45: Joseph reveals himself: 'You meant evil, but God meant it for good' — doctrine of providence in capsule.
Gen.46: Jacob's family (seventy souls) descends to Egypt under God's direction — Exodus seedbed planted.
Gen.47: Joseph saves Egypt and Israel through famine policy; Jacob blesses Pharaoh.
Gen.48: Jacob blesses Ephraim and Manasseh, crossing hands — sovereign election overrides primogeniture.
Gen.49: Jacob's prophetic blessings: Shiloh from Judah — Messianic scepter promise central.
Gen.50: Joseph's death; bones to be carried up — eyes fixed on the promised land and resurrection hope.
Exod.1: Israel multiplies in Egypt; new Pharaoh enslaves them; midwives fear God and are blessed.
Exod.2: Moses born, hidden, drawn from the Nile; flees to Midian — preparation of the deliverer.
Exod.3: Burning bush; the divine name 'I AM' (YHWH) revealed; Moses commissioned to lead the Exodus.
Exod.4: Signs given to Moses; Zipporah's circumcision crisis — covenant sign demanded of the deliverer's house.
Exod.5: Moses confronts Pharaoh; bricks without straw; suffering deepens before deliverance.
Exod.6: God reaffirms covenant: 'I will be your God'; redemption rooted in His name and promise to the patriarchs.
Exod.7: Plagues begin: rod to serpent, Nile to blood; YHWH wages war on Egypt's gods.
Exod.8: Frogs, gnats, flies — Pharaoh's hardening progresses; God distinguishes Goshen.
Exod.9: Livestock, boils, hail — judgments escalate; God's purpose: 'that My name may be proclaimed in all the earth.'
Exod.10: Locusts and darkness; Pharaoh's heart hardened by God's judicial decree.
Exod.11: Final plague announced — death of the firstborn; Israel's distinction made absolute.
Exod.12: Passover instituted: lamb slain, blood applied, judgment passes over — typology of Christ our Passover (1 Cor 5:7).
Exod.13: Consecration of the firstborn; pillar of cloud and fire — God leads His redeemed people.
Exod.14: Red Sea crossed; Egypt's army drowned — definitive deliverance and baptismal type (1 Cor 10).
Exod.15: Song of Moses — first hymn of redemption; God praised as warrior and king forever.
Exod.16: Manna from heaven — daily bread teaching dependence; sabbath pattern emerges.
Exod.17: Water from the rock (Christ-type, 1 Cor 10:4); Amalek defeated as Moses' hands are upheld.
Exod.18: Jethro's counsel — delegated leadership; Gentile wisdom serving covenant administration.
Exod.19: Sinai theophany; Israel set apart as 'kingdom of priests, holy nation'; covenant prologue.
Exod.20: The Decalogue at Sinai — God's moral law in two tables (love God / love neighbor), the covenantal heart of Israel's identity.
Exod.21: Case laws on slavery, violence, and damages — Decalogue applied to civil life.
Exod.22: Property and social laws; protection of the vulnerable rooted in God's character.
Exod.23: Justice, sabbaths, feasts; angel of the Lord promised to lead Israel into the land.
Exod.24: Covenant ratified by blood; elders see God and eat — foretaste of communion and the new covenant meal.
Exod.25: Tabernacle pattern given: ark, table, lampstand — heavenly realities sketched on earth (Heb 8:5).
Exod.26: Tabernacle structure — curtains, frames, veil; God dwells with His people in graded holiness.
Exod.27: Bronze altar and courtyard — sacrifice precedes presence; access requires blood.
Exod.28: Priestly garments for Aaron — glory and beauty for mediation; names of tribes borne on heart and shoulders.
Exod.29: Consecration of priests by blood and oil; daily sacrifices — perpetual atonement and presence.
Exod.30: Incense altar, atonement money, laver, anointing oil — worship's holiness safeguarded.
Exod.31: Bezalel filled with Spirit for craftsmanship; sabbath as covenant sign reaffirmed.
Exod.32: Golden calf apostasy; Moses intercedes; Levites zealous — sin's depth and mediator's role.
Exod.33: Moses pleads for God's presence — 'If your presence does not go with us...'; God's glory promised.
Exod.34: Covenant renewed; God proclaims His name — merciful, gracious, just; Moses' face shines.
Exod.35: Sabbath reaffirmed; freewill offerings for tabernacle — willing-hearted worship.
Exod.36: Construction begins; people give more than needed — generosity overflows.
Exod.37: Bezalel makes ark, table, lampstand, incense altar — heavenly furniture fashioned.
Exod.38: Bronze altar, laver, courtyard built; inventory of materials.
Exod.39: Priestly garments completed exactly as commanded — obedience as worship.
Exod.40: Tabernacle erected; glory cloud descends — God dwells with His people, Exodus' goal achieved.
Lev.1: Burnt offering — wholly consumed, picturing total consecration and substitutionary atonement.
Lev.2: Grain offering — fruit of labor offered with frankincense; no leaven, salt of covenant.
Lev.3: Peace offering — fellowship meal sealing reconciliation with God.
Lev.4: Sin offering for unintentional sin — atonement available even for inadvertent guilt.
Lev.5: Trespass offering with restitution — sin against God demands both blood and reparation.
Lev.6: Further laws on offerings; perpetual fire on the altar — atonement never to be extinguished.
Lev.7: Priestly portions and prohibitions; communion meal regulated.
Lev.8: Aaron and sons consecrated — blood applied to ear, hand, foot: total devotion.
Lev.9: Priesthood inaugurated; fire from the LORD consumes the offering — divine acceptance.
Lev.10: Nadab and Abihu offer strange fire and die — God will be sanctified by those who draw near.
Lev.11: Clean and unclean animals — Israel's diet sets them apart as holy.
Lev.12: Purification after childbirth — even life's beginnings touched by post-Fall uncleanness.
Lev.13: Diagnosis of skin disease — priest as discerner of clean and unclean.
Lev.14: Cleansing of the leper — two birds, blood and living water; type of gospel restoration.
Lev.15: Cleanness laws on bodily discharges — life's fluids and holiness.
Lev.16: Day of Atonement — high priest enters the holy of holies; scapegoat carries sin away (Heb 9-10 fulfillment).
Lev.17: Blood prohibition; centralized sacrifice — 'the life is in the blood,' atonement's heart.
Lev.18: Sexual ethics — boundaries against Canaanite abominations; holiness in the body.
Lev.19: Holiness code: 'Be holy, for I am holy'; love of neighbor (19:18) summarized by Christ.
Lev.20: Capital sanctions for covenant abominations — purging evil to preserve holiness.
Lev.21: Priestly holiness — higher standards for those who handle holy things.
Lev.22: Holy offerings to be handled with reverence; unblemished sacrifices required.
Lev.23: Liturgical calendar — sabbaths, Passover, Pentecost, Tabernacles; redemptive history rehearsed annually.
Lev.24: Lampstand, showbread; blasphemer stoned — God's name guarded among the people.
Lev.25: Sabbath year and Jubilee — economic reset and redemption motifs anchoring social justice.
Lev.26: Blessings and curses — covenant sanctions; exile foreseen but restoration promised.
Lev.27: Vows and dedications; tithes — voluntary offerings under regulated holiness.
Num.1: First census — Israel mustered as YHWH's army for the wilderness march.
Num.2: Tribal arrangement around the tabernacle — God at the center of His people.
Num.3: Levites set apart in place of firstborn — substitutionary service.
Num.4: Kohathite, Gershonite, Merarite duties — holy things handled with care.
Num.5: Camp purity, restitution, jealousy ordeal — holiness in marriage and community.
Num.6: Nazirite vow; Aaronic blessing — separation unto God and benediction of His face.
Num.7: Tribal offerings for tabernacle dedication — twelvefold generosity.
Num.8: Lampstand lit; Levites consecrated as living offering.
Num.9: Second Passover; cloud guides Israel — worship and walk under divine direction.
Num.10: Trumpets; departure from Sinai — pilgrim march begins.
Num.11: Complaints, quail, seventy elders Spirit-anointed — leadership and grumbling judged.
Num.12: Miriam and Aaron oppose Moses — God vindicates His chosen mediator.
Num.13: Twelve spies sent; ten bring an evil report — sight without faith breeds fear.
Num.14: Israel rebels; wilderness generation condemned — unbelief bars from rest (Heb 3-4).
Num.15: Offering laws; Sabbath-breaker stoned; tassels as memorial of commandments.
Num.16: Korah's rebellion — earth swallows usurpers; only God's chosen priest stands.
Num.17: Aaron's rod buds — divine vindication of priesthood by miraculous life.
Num.18: Priestly and Levitical duties and dues — God Himself their inheritance.
Num.19: Red heifer — water of purification for defilement by death (cf. Heb 9:13-14).
Num.20: Moses strikes the rock; Miriam and Aaron die — leaders barred from Canaan for unbelief.
Num.21: Bronze serpent — look and live, fulfilled in Christ lifted up (Jn 3:14).
Num.22: Balaam summoned; donkey rebukes prophet — God controls even hostile mouths.
Num.23: Balaam's first oracles — he can only bless what God has blessed.
Num.24: Balaam sees a Star out of Jacob — Messianic prophecy from a pagan seer.
Num.25: Baal-Peor apostasy; Phinehas's zeal halts plague — covenant of perpetual priesthood.
Num.26: Second census — new generation prepared for conquest.
Num.27: Daughters of Zelophehad; Joshua commissioned as Moses' successor.
Num.28: Daily, weekly, monthly offerings — rhythm of perpetual worship.
Num.29: Festal offerings — Trumpets, Atonement, Tabernacles regulated.
Num.30: Vows of women and men — solemnity of the spoken word before God.
Num.31: War on Midian — judgment for Peor; spoils sanctified.
Num.32: Reuben, Gad, half-Manasseh request Transjordan — pledge to fight for brethren first.
Num.33: Itinerary of the wilderness journey — God's faithfulness recorded stage by stage.
Num.34: Land boundaries — promised inheritance mapped before crossing.
Num.35: Levitical cities and cities of refuge — justice and mercy structured.
Num.36: Inheritance of Zelophehad's daughters secured within tribe — preserving tribal allotments.
Deut.1: Moses recounts Sinai-to-Kadesh; failure at the spies — covenant memory shapes covenant future.
Deut.2: Wilderness wanderings reviewed; God's gifts of land to Edom, Moab, Ammon respected.
Deut.3: Defeat of Og and Sihon; Joshua to lead — Moses' sight of the land but not entry.
Deut.4: Call to obedience: 'Keep the statutes' — uniqueness of YHWH and His revelation.
Deut.5: Decalogue restated for the new generation — covenant continuity affirmed.
Deut.6: Shema: 'Hear, O Israel: YHWH our God, YHWH is one' — total love of God; foundational creed.
Deut.7: Election love: 'because the LORD loved you'; herem warfare against Canaanite idolatry.
Deut.8: Wilderness as discipline; warning against pride — 'man does not live by bread alone.'
Deut.9: Israel reminded of their stubbornness — possession not by righteousness but by grace.
Deut.10: Renewed tablets; circumcision of the heart called for — true covenant religion is inward.
Deut.11: Blessings and curses; Ebal and Gerizim foreshadowed; love and obey or perish.
Deut.12: Central sanctuary — worship only where God places His name; idolatry rooted out.
Deut.13: False prophets and apostate cities — first commandment guarded by capital sanction.
Deut.14: Clean/unclean revisited; tithes — Israel as God's holy children.
Deut.15: Sabbath year debt release; care for the poor — gospel pattern of generosity.
Deut.16: Pilgrim feasts: Passover, Weeks, Tabernacles — appearing before the Lord.
Deut.17: Judges, courts, and the law of the king — limited monarchy under Torah.
Deut.18: Priestly portions; the prophet like Moses (18:15) — Messianic prophecy applied to Christ (Acts 3).
Deut.19: Cities of refuge and witnesses — justice tempered with mercy.
Deut.20: Laws of warfare — herem, exemptions; God fights for His people.
Deut.21: Atonement for unsolved murder; firstborn rights; hanged man cursed (Gal 3:13 fulfilled in Christ).
Deut.22: Various civil laws — neighbor love applied to property, sex, and dress.
Deut.23: Assembly exclusions; vows; cleanliness in camp — holiness of the covenant community.
Deut.24: Divorce, pledges, kindness to vulnerable — humane application of law.
Deut.25: Limits on punishment; levirate marriage; Amalek to be blotted out.
Deut.26: Firstfruits liturgy and tithe confession — covenant memory in worship.
Deut.27: Stones on Ebal; covenant curses pronounced — law's verdict on disobedience.
Deut.28: Blessings and curses in detail — covenant sanctions central to Israel's history.
Deut.29: Covenant in Moab renewed — secret things to God, revealed things to us.
Deut.30: Choose life; circumcision of heart promised; word near you (Rom 10:6-8 cites this).
Deut.31: Joshua commissioned; law to be read every seven years; song of witness given.
Deut.32: Song of Moses — God's faithfulness, Israel's apostasy, future judgment and mercy.
Deut.33: Moses' tribal blessings — final benediction over Israel.
Deut.34: Moses dies on Nebo; sees the land; 'no prophet like Moses' until Christ.
Josh.1: Joshua commissioned: 'Be strong and courageous'; Word of God as foundation for conquest.
Josh.2: Rahab hides spies; faith of a Gentile prostitute joined to Israel (Heb 11; Matt 1).
Josh.3: Jordan parted by ark — new Exodus into the inheritance.
Josh.4: Twelve memorial stones — covenant memory passed to children.
Josh.5: Circumcision and Passover at Gilgal; commander of the LORD's army appears.
Josh.6: Jericho falls by faith and trumpet — God gives the city; Rahab spared.
Josh.7: Achan's sin defeats Israel at Ai — covenant solidarity and corporate guilt.
Josh.8: Ai destroyed; covenant renewal at Ebal/Gerizim — law read on the land.
Josh.9: Gibeonite deception — Israel fails to inquire; covenant honored despite trickery.
Josh.10: Sun stands still; southern coalition crushed — God fights for Israel.
Josh.11: Northern conquest under Hazor; 'Joshua took the whole land' as God commanded.
Josh.12: Catalogue of defeated kings — testimony to God's promise-keeping.
Josh.13: Land yet to be possessed; Transjordan allotments — promise outpacing realization.
Josh.14: Caleb's bold claim of Hebron at 85 — wholehearted faith rewarded.
Josh.15: Judah's allotment — largest tribal inheritance with Caleb's portion.
Josh.16: Ephraim's territory; failure to drive out Canaanites noted.
Josh.17: Manasseh's allotment; Zelophehad's daughters honored; complaints answered.
Josh.18: Tabernacle set up at Shiloh; remaining tribes allotted.
Josh.19: Final tribal allotments; Joshua's own portion last — servant leadership.
Josh.20: Cities of refuge appointed — mercy structured into justice.
Josh.21: Levitical cities given; 'not one word failed of all the good promises' (21:45).
Josh.22: Eastern tribes' altar nearly causes war; misunderstanding clarified — unity preserved.
Josh.23: Joshua's farewell: hold fast to God; warning against intermarriage and apostasy.
Josh.24: Covenant renewal at Shechem: 'Choose this day' — 'as for me and my house, we will serve the LORD.'
Judg.1: Incomplete conquest; tribes fail to drive out Canaanites — seeds of compromise sown.
Judg.2: Cycle introduced: sin, servitude, supplication, salvation; Israel's recurring apostasy.
Judg.3: Othniel, Ehud, Shamgar — first deliverers raised by God's Spirit.
Judg.4: Deborah and Barak; Jael drives the tent peg — God uses unlikely vessels for victory.
Judg.5: Song of Deborah — poetic celebration of God's triumph.
Judg.6: Gideon called from threshing floor; fleece tested — God meets weak faith with patience.
Judg.7: Three hundred defeat Midian — 'lest Israel boast'; victory by sovereign weakness.
Judg.8: Gideon's later compromise: ephod becomes a snare; idolatry creeps in.
Judg.9: Abimelech's bloody usurpation; Jotham's parable; tyranny self-destructs.
Judg.10: Tola, Jair; Israel's deepening idolatry; God's grieved patience.
Judg.11: Jephthah delivers Ammon; rash vow regarding his daughter — folly amid faith.
Judg.12: Shibboleth incident; tribal civil strife — covenant fraying.
Judg.13: Samson's birth foretold; angelic theophany; Nazirite from the womb.
Judg.14: Samson's Philistine bride; riddle and revenge — Spirit's strength misused.
Judg.15: Samson's exploits with jawbone; he judges Israel under Philistine oppression.
Judg.16: Delilah; Samson's fall and redemptive death — strength returns at the end.
Judg.17: Micah's idol and personal Levite — 'every man did what was right in his own eyes.'
Judg.18: Danites take Micah's idol and city — institutionalized apostasy.
Judg.19: Levite's concubine outraged at Gibeah — Sodom-like depravity within Israel.
Judg.20: Civil war against Benjamin — Israel nearly self-destructs.
Judg.21: Wives for surviving Benjamites; book closes: 'no king in Israel' — longing for true king.
Ruth.1: Famine drives Elimelech to Moab; Naomi returns bitter; Ruth's covenant pledge: 'your God will be my God.'
Ruth.2: Ruth gleans in Boaz's field; providential favor — kinsman-redeemer introduced.
Ruth.3: Threshing floor proposal; Ruth seeks redemption under Boaz's wing.
Ruth.4: Boaz redeems Ruth; Obed born — line to David and ultimately Christ; Gentile bride grafted in.
1Sam.1: Hannah's barrenness and prayer; Samuel born and dedicated — God hears the lowly.
1Sam.2: Hannah's Magnificat-like song; Eli's wicked sons; coming judgment on the priesthood.
1Sam.3: Samuel's call: 'Speak, for your servant hears'; prophetic word reawakens.
1Sam.4: Ark captured; Eli dies; Ichabod ('glory departed') — presence cannot be presumed upon.
1Sam.5: Dagon falls before the ark; Philistines plagued — YHWH's supremacy among the gods.
1Sam.6: Ark returned on a cart; Beth-Shemesh struck for irreverence.
1Sam.7: Samuel judges; Ebenezer raised: 'Hitherto has the LORD helped us.'
1Sam.8: Israel demands a king like the nations — rejecting God's kingship.
1Sam.9: Saul introduced; providential meeting with Samuel arranged by God.
1Sam.10: Saul anointed; Spirit comes upon him; chosen by lot before the people.
1Sam.11: Saul rescues Jabesh-Gilead; kingship confirmed at Gilgal.
1Sam.12: Samuel's farewell; thunder confirms warning; 'fear the LORD and serve Him.'
1Sam.13: Saul's unlawful sacrifice; kingdom torn from his line — obedience over ritual.
1Sam.14: Jonathan's faith-filled raid; Saul's rash oath nearly kills his son.
1Sam.15: Saul spares Agag; Samuel: 'to obey is better than sacrifice'; Saul rejected as king.
1Sam.16: David anointed: 'man looks on outward, God looks on the heart'; Spirit comes on David.
1Sam.17: David vs Goliath. The shepherd boy's confidence is theological, not athletic: 'the battle is the LORD's' — a Christ-typing victory by an unlikely champion.
1Sam.18: Jonathan's covenant love for David; Saul's jealousy ignites.
1Sam.19: Saul tries to kill David; Michal and Jonathan protect him; God shields His anointed.
1Sam.20: Jonathan and David's covenant arrows; friendship under persecution.
1Sam.21: David at Nob eats showbread (Mt 12:3); flees to Gath, feigns madness.
1Sam.22: Cave of Adullam; Doeg slaughters priests at Saul's command.
1Sam.23: David inquires of God repeatedly; Keilah delivered; wilderness flight continues.
1Sam.24: David spares Saul in the cave — refuses to harm the LORD's anointed.
1Sam.25: Nabal and Abigail; David restrained from vengeance by a wise woman.
1Sam.26: David spares Saul a second time at Hachilah — vengeance belongs to the LORD.
1Sam.27: David flees to Ziklag under Achish — low point of compromise.
1Sam.28: Saul consults the medium of Endor; Samuel's specter pronounces doom.
1Sam.29: Philistine commanders reject David from the battle — God's providence.
1Sam.30: Ziklag burned; David strengthens himself in the LORD; recovers all.
1Sam.31: Saul falls on Mt. Gilboa with his sons — house of Saul ends in defeat.
2Sam.1: David laments Saul and Jonathan: 'How the mighty have fallen' — magnanimity in grief.
2Sam.2: David crowned in Hebron over Judah; Ish-bosheth contests in the north.
2Sam.3: Abner defects to David; murdered by Joab — David grieves publicly.
2Sam.4: Ish-bosheth assassinated; David executes the murderers — refusing to profit from bloodshed.
2Sam.5: David crowned over all Israel; takes Jerusalem — Zion the city of David.
2Sam.6: Ark brought to Jerusalem; Uzzah struck for irreverence; David dances; Michal despises him.
2Sam.7: Davidic covenant: God will build David a house — Messianic throne forever (fulfilled in Christ).
2Sam.8: David's victories; kingdom established and secured on every side.
2Sam.9: Mephibosheth shown hesed for Jonathan's sake — covenant kindness to the lame.
2Sam.10: Ammonite war — David's mercy spurned; God grants victory.
2Sam.11: David, Bathsheba, and Uriah — adultery and murder; covenant king's deepest fall.
2Sam.12: Nathan's parable; 'You are the man'; David repents (Ps 51); sword promised on his house.
2Sam.13: Amnon rapes Tamar; Absalom's vengeance — sin's ripple in David's house.
2Sam.14: Joab and Tekoa woman bring Absalom back — partial reconciliation.
2Sam.15: Absalom's revolt; David flees Jerusalem barefoot — chastening foretold by Nathan.
2Sam.16: Shimei curses; Ahithophel counsels Absalom — David bears reproach.
2Sam.17: Hushai outwits Ahithophel — God turns the counsel of the wicked to folly.
2Sam.18: Absalom slain in oak tree; David weeps: 'O my son Absalom!'
2Sam.19: David restored; Shimei spared; Mephibosheth vindicated; tribal jealousy.
2Sam.20: Sheba's revolt; Joab kills Amasa — kingdom unity strained.
2Sam.21: Famine for Saul's bloodguilt avenged; Gibeonites satisfied; David's mighty men.
2Sam.22: David's song of deliverance — God his rock, fortress, and savior (parallels Ps 18).
2Sam.23: David's last words; mighty men listed — covenant of God 'ordered in all things and sure.'
2Sam.24: Census and plague; David buys Araunah's threshing floor — future temple site.
1Kgs.1: Adonijah usurps; Bathsheba and Nathan secure Solomon's coronation.
1Kgs.2: David's charge; Solomon consolidates throne; Joab and Shimei executed.
1Kgs.3: Solomon at Gibeon asks for wisdom; judges between two mothers — wisdom from above.
1Kgs.4: Solomon's administration; Israel and Judah at peace under fig and vine.
1Kgs.5: Hiram of Tyre supplies cedar for the temple — international cooperation in building God's house.
1Kgs.6: Temple constructed in seven years — 480 years after the Exodus; God's promise embodied.
1Kgs.7: Solomon's palace and temple furnishings — Bezalel-style craftsmanship.
1Kgs.8: Temple dedicated; Solomon's prayer; glory cloud fills the house — God dwells with Israel.
1Kgs.9: Covenant warning to Solomon; second appearance of God; hint of future exile if disobedient.
1Kgs.10: Queen of Sheba visits; 'a greater than Solomon is here' (Mt 12:42).
1Kgs.11: Solomon's many wives turn his heart; kingdom to be torn — apostasy at the height of glory.
1Kgs.12: Rehoboam's folly; Jeroboam revolts; kingdom divided; golden calves at Dan and Bethel.
1Kgs.13: Man of God from Judah; warning to Jeroboam; lion judgment for disobedient prophet.
1Kgs.14: Jeroboam's house judged; Rehoboam's idolatry; Shishak plunders Jerusalem.
1Kgs.15: Asa reforms in Judah; Nadab and Baasha in apostate Israel.
1Kgs.16: Northern dynasties churn; Omri and Ahab — depths of Israelite apostasy.
1Kgs.17: Elijah introduced; ravens, widow of Zarephath, raises her son — prophet of life.
1Kgs.18: Mt. Carmel: fire falls; 'YHWH, He is God!' — Baalism publicly defeated.
1Kgs.19: Elijah flees Jezebel; still small voice at Horeb; Elisha called.
1Kgs.20: Ahab defeats Ben-hadad twice; spares him — judgment pronounced.
1Kgs.21: Naboth's vineyard; Jezebel's murder by judicial fraud; doom on Ahab's house.
1Kgs.22: Micaiah's true prophecy; Ahab dies at Ramoth-gilead — dogs lick his blood.
2Kgs.1: Ahaziah consults Baal-zebub; Elijah calls down fire; king dies under judgment.
2Kgs.2: Elijah taken in a whirlwind; double portion to Elisha; ministry continues.
2Kgs.3: Moab rebels; Jehoshaphat allies with Joram; victory through Elisha's word.
2Kgs.4: Widow's oil multiplied; Shunammite's son raised — prophet brings life.
2Kgs.5: Naaman the Syrian healed in the Jordan — Gentile faith, Gehazi's greed cursed (Lk 4:27).
2Kgs.6: Floating axe head; chariots of fire — 'those with us are more than those with them.'
2Kgs.7: Lepers find empty Aramean camp; siege ends overnight by God's word.
2Kgs.8: Hazael's rise foretold; Edom revolts; Judah's apostate kings continue.
2Kgs.9: Jehu anointed; Joram and Ahaziah killed; Jezebel thrown down — prophecy fulfilled.
2Kgs.10: Jehu purges Baal worship and Ahab's house; later compromises with calf cult.
2Kgs.11: Athaliah's tyranny; Joash hidden, then crowned by Jehoiada — Davidic line preserved.
2Kgs.12: Joash repairs the temple; later turns away after Jehoiada's death.
2Kgs.13: Jehoahaz and Jehoash; Elisha dies; corpse touching his bones revives — prophetic power lingers.
2Kgs.14: Amaziah of Judah; Jeroboam II expands Israel — outward prosperity, inner decay.
2Kgs.15: Six kings in succession; northern instability; Assyrian shadow lengthens.
2Kgs.16: Ahaz of Judah — Assyrian-style altar; deep apostasy in the south.
2Kgs.17: Samaria falls to Assyria; northern kingdom exiled; reasons given — covenant unfaithfulness.
2Kgs.18: Hezekiah's reforms; Sennacherib invades; Rabshakeh blasphemes.
2Kgs.19: Hezekiah prays; Isaiah prophesies; angel slays 185,000 Assyrians overnight.
2Kgs.20: Hezekiah healed; sun's shadow retreats; foolish display to Babylonian envoys.
2Kgs.21: Manasseh's atrocities — 'sins for which the LORD would not pardon'; Amon follows.
2Kgs.22: Josiah finds the Book of the Law; tears clothes; reform begins.
2Kgs.23: Josiah's sweeping reforms; Passover renewed; killed at Megiddo.
2Kgs.24: Jehoiakim and Jehoiachin; first deportations to Babylon — covenant curses falling.
2Kgs.25: Jerusalem falls; temple burned; Zedekiah blinded; Jehoiachin freed in exile — flicker of hope.
1Chr.1: Genealogy from Adam to the patriarchs — universal history narrowed to Israel.
1Chr.2: Genealogy of Judah — David's line traced.
1Chr.3: Davidic descendants through and beyond exile — royal line preserved.
1Chr.4: Judah and Simeon genealogies; Jabez prays for blessing and is heard.
1Chr.6: Levi's descendants — priests and singers organized for worship.
1Chr.7: Genealogies of Issachar, Benjamin, Naphtali, Manasseh, Ephraim, Asher.
1Chr.8: Benjamin's line, leading to Saul — context for kingship transition.
1Chr.9: Returnees to Jerusalem and temple servants listed — post-exilic restoration framed.
1Chr.10: Saul's death — narrated as judgment for unfaithfulness.
1Chr.11: David crowned at Hebron; Jerusalem captured; mighty men named.
1Chr.12: Warriors who joined David — God's providential gathering of his army.
1Chr.13: Ark moved improperly; Uzzah dies — holiness must be approached on God's terms.
1Chr.14: David's house built; Philistines defeated; God's blessing publicized.
1Chr.15: Ark brought up properly by Levites; David dances — worship rightly ordered.
1Chr.16: Psalm of thanks at the ark — central worship celebrated.
1Chr.17: Davidic covenant restated — eternal throne promised, fulfilled in Christ.
1Chr.18: David's victories; tribute flows in; kingdom extended.
1Chr.19: Ammonite war; mercenaries hired and crushed.
1Chr.20: Rabbah captured; Philistine giants slain — Bathsheba episode omitted by Chronicler's liturgical focus.
1Chr.21: Census; plague; Araunah's threshing floor — site of future temple secured.
1Chr.22: David prepares materials for the temple; charges Solomon to build.
1Chr.23: Levites organized for service — worship structured for the new house.
1Chr.24: Priestly divisions established — twenty-four courses (cf. Lk 1:5).
1Chr.25: Singers and musicians appointed — prophetic worship under David.
1Chr.26: Gatekeepers and treasurers — every detail of temple life ordered.
1Chr.27: Military and civil administrators — kingdom governance structured.
1Chr.28: David's charge to Solomon and Israel: build the temple; God-given pattern.
1Chr.29: Freewill offerings; David's doxology: 'all things come from You'; Solomon enthroned.
2Chr.1: Solomon at Gibeon asks for wisdom — granted with riches and honor besides.
2Chr.2: Solomon contracts Hiram; preparations to build a house worthy of God.
2Chr.3: Temple construction on Mt. Moriah — site of Abraham's offering, David's altar.
2Chr.4: Temple furnishings — bronze sea, lampstands, altars; symbolism of holiness.
2Chr.5: Ark installed; glory cloud fills temple — God dwells in Zion.
2Chr.6: Solomon's dedicatory prayer — temple as place of intercession for sin and exile.
2Chr.7: Fire consumes the sacrifice; 'if my people humble themselves' — covenant response promised.
2Chr.8: Solomon's building projects and trade; worship maintained according to David's order.
2Chr.9: Queen of Sheba; Solomon's wealth; foreshadowing of nations streaming to Zion.
2Chr.10: Rehoboam's folly splits the kingdom — Chronicler focuses thereafter on Judah.
2Chr.11: Rehoboam fortifies cities; Levites flee north to Judah for true worship.
2Chr.12: Shishak invades because of unfaithfulness; humbling brings partial mercy.
2Chr.13: Abijah's speech: God is with Judah's true priesthood; Jeroboam's army defeated.
2Chr.14: Asa's reforms and victory over Zerah's Cushites — relying on the LORD.
2Chr.15: Azariah's prophecy spurs deeper covenant renewal under Asa.
2Chr.16: Asa's later alliance with Syria; rebuked; dies of foot disease — pragmatism over faith.
2Chr.17: Jehoshaphat's reform; Levites teach the Law throughout Judah.
2Chr.18: Jehoshaphat allies with Ahab; Micaiah's prophecy; Ahab dies as foretold.
2Chr.19: Jehoshaphat rebuked; appoints judges: 'judge for the LORD.'
2Chr.20: Coalition invades; Jehoshaphat prays; 'the battle is not yours but God's' — singers lead the army.
2Chr.21: Jehoram's apostasy; Elijah's letter; bowel disease judgment.
2Chr.22: Ahaziah and Athaliah — Davidic line nearly extinguished but preserved by Jehosheba.
2Chr.23: Jehoiada crowns Joash; Athaliah killed — covenant restored.
2Chr.24: Joash repairs temple; after Jehoiada dies, kills Zechariah the prophet — apostasy returns.
2Chr.25: Amaziah defeats Edom but adopts their gods — judgment by Israel.
2Chr.26: Uzziah's prosperity; usurps priestly role and is struck with leprosy.
2Chr.27: Jotham reigns rightly — brief faithful interlude.
2Chr.28: Ahaz's deep apostasy; Israel returns Judah's captives at prophet's word.
2Chr.29: Hezekiah cleanses and reconsecrates the temple — worship restored.
2Chr.30: Great Passover celebrated; northern remnant invited; mercy on the unprepared.
2Chr.31: Tithes flow in; Levitical service ordered — reform bears fruit.
2Chr.32: Sennacherib's invasion; Hezekiah's prayer; angel destroys Assyrian army.
2Chr.33: Manasseh's wickedness; humbled in Babylon, repents — grace for the chief of sinners.
2Chr.34: Josiah's reforms; Book of the Law found; covenant renewed.
2Chr.35: Greatest Passover since Samuel; Josiah killed at Megiddo by Neco.
2Chr.36: Final kings; Jerusalem falls; seventy-year exile; Cyrus's decree — book ends with hope of return.
Ezra.1: Cyrus's decree (fulfilling Jeremiah) sends exiles home; God stirs hearts to rebuild.
Ezra.2: List of returnees — covenant remnant named and counted.
Ezra.3: Altar rebuilt; foundation of temple laid; mixed weeping and joy.
Ezra.4: Adversaries oppose the work; rebuilding halted under Artaxerxes.
Ezra.5: Haggai and Zechariah stir the people; work resumes; Tattenai inquires.
Ezra.6: Cyrus's decree confirmed by Darius; temple completed and dedicated; Passover kept.
Ezra.7: Ezra the scribe sent with royal authority — 'set his heart to study and do the Law.'
Ezra.8: Ezra's caravan; fast for safe journey; God's hand on them.
Ezra.9: Mixed marriages exposed; Ezra's prayer of corporate confession.
Ezra.10: Reform: foreign wives put away to safeguard covenant identity (cf. Mal 2).
Neh.1: News of Jerusalem's ruin; Nehemiah weeps and prays — confession and covenant appeal.
Neh.2: Artaxerxes commissions Nehemiah; secret survey of the walls; 'the God of heaven will give us success.'
Neh.3: Builders organized at the wall — every family and trade contributing.
Neh.4: Sanballat mocks; Nehemiah prays and posts guards — sword and trowel.
Neh.5: Internal injustice rebuked; Nehemiah forgoes governor's allowance — leadership integrity.
Neh.6: Plots to derail Nehemiah; wall finished in fifty-two days — God's hand evident.
Neh.7: Census of returnees mirrors Ezra 2 — covenant continuity.
Neh.8: Ezra reads the Law at Water Gate; people weep then rejoice — Word central to renewal.
Neh.9: Levitical prayer recounting redemption history; covenant renewed in confession.
Neh.10: Sealed pledge to obey Torah, support temple, observe sabbath — covenant in action.
Neh.11: Population of Jerusalem; lots cast to repopulate the holy city.
Neh.12: Wall dedication with double choirs; joy heard from afar.
Neh.13: Final reforms: temple abuses, sabbath breaches, mixed marriages — vigilance against backsliding.
Esth.1: Vashti deposed at Susa; stage set for providential rise of Esther.
Esth.2: Esther chosen queen; Mordecai foils assassination plot — quiet providence.
Esth.3: Haman's pride and decree to annihilate Jews — covenant people imperiled.
Esth.4: Mordecai's plea: 'for such a time as this'; Esther resolves: 'if I perish, I perish.'
Esth.5: Esther's banquet; Haman builds gallows for Mordecai — pride before fall.
Esth.6: Sleepless king honors Mordecai; Haman forced to lead him in triumph — irony of providence.
Esth.7: Haman exposed at the second banquet; hanged on his own gallows.
Esth.8: Counter-decree allows Jews to defend themselves; salvation in the eleventh hour.
Esth.9: Jews triumph over enemies; Purim instituted — feast of deliverance.
Esth.10: Mordecai exalted; though God's name is never spoken, His hand governs every page.
Job.1: Job's prosperity, the heavenly council with Satan, and the catastrophic loss — the suffering of a righteous man under divine permission.
Job.2: Second assault: Job's body afflicted with sores; his wife counsels cursing God; three friends arrive and sit silent seven days.
Job.3: Job breaks silence, cursing the day of his birth — lament that probes the mystery of suffering without abandoning God.
Job.4: Eliphaz's first speech: a vision in the night argues no mortal is righteous before God; insinuates Job's guilt.
Job.5: Eliphaz urges Job to seek God; the chastening of the Almighty is blessed — retribution theology assumed.
Job.6: Job answers: his grief is heavier than the sand; rebukes friends as faithless brooks that fail in drought.
Job.7: Job laments human frailty before God; cries out, 'What is man that you make so much of him?'
Job.8: Bildad's first speech: God does not pervert justice; the past generations confirm the wicked perish.
Job.9: Job concedes God's sovereignty but longs for an arbiter to mediate between him and the Almighty.
Job.10: Job pleads with God to show why He contends with him; the Creator now seems the destroyer.
Job.11: Zophar's first speech: rebukes Job's words; God's wisdom is unsearchable; urges repentance for restoration.
Job.12: Job mocks the friends' wisdom; declares God's sovereignty over rulers, nations, and the natural order.
Job.13: Job appeals from friends to God Himself: 'Though he slay me, yet will I trust in him.'
Job.14: Man's days are few and full of trouble; Job ponders whether the dead live again — a flicker of resurrection hope.
Job.15: Eliphaz's second speech: accuses Job of pride; the wicked are tormented all their days.
Job.16: Job calls friends 'miserable comforters'; affirms a witness in heaven who pleads his cause.
Job.17: Job's spirit is broken; the grave is his home; he asks where his hope can be found.
Job.18: Bildad's second speech: a graphic portrait of the doom that overtakes the wicked.
Job.19: Job's great confession: 'I know that my Redeemer lives' — bedrock messianic and resurrection hope.
Job.20: Zophar's second speech: the triumph of the wicked is brief; their prosperity vanishes like a dream.
Job.21: Job rebuts: in fact the wicked often prosper, undermining the friends' simplistic retribution doctrine.
Job.22: Eliphaz's third speech: falsely catalogues Job's sins; promises blessing if he returns to God.
Job.23: Job longs to find God to plead his case: 'He knows the way I take; when he has tried me, I shall come forth as gold.'
Job.24: Job questions why God does not set times of judgment; the wicked oppress the poor seemingly unpunished.
Job.25: Bildad's brief third speech: man cannot be righteous before God — a maggot, a worm.
Job.26: Job extols God's majesty over creation; these are but the outskirts of his ways.
Job.27: Job maintains his integrity; he will not concede the friends' charges; the wicked's portion is destruction.
Job.28: The hymn to wisdom: men mine for jewels, but the fear of the LORD is true wisdom; departing evil is understanding.
Job.29: Job recalls his former honor and benevolence — the days when God's friendship rested on his tent.
Job.30: Now Job is mocked by the lowest of men; his harp is turned to mourning.
Job.31: Job's oath of innocence — a comprehensive ethic of personal righteousness as final defense.
Job.32: Elihu, young and angry, enters the dialogue, claiming Spirit-given understanding apart from age.
Job.33: Elihu argues God speaks through dreams and suffering to discipline and ransom the soul from the pit.
Job.34: Elihu defends God's perfect justice — the Almighty does no wickedness.
Job.35: Elihu: human righteousness adds nothing to God; sin harms only fellow men.
Job.36: Elihu exalts God's greatness in justice and chastening; affliction opens the ear to instruction.
Job.37: Elihu marvels at God in storm, snow, and lightning — a prelude to the Lord's own appearing.
Job.38: The LORD answers from the whirlwind: 'Where were you when I laid the foundations of the earth?'
Job.39: God parades the wonders of the animal kingdom — wild donkey, ostrich, war horse, eagle — beyond Job's comprehension.
Job.40: God challenges Job to gird up his loins; Job lays his hand on his mouth. Behemoth introduced.
Job.41: Leviathan: an untamable creature displaying God's overwhelming power — none dare rouse him; how then stand before God?
Job.42: Job repents in dust and ashes; God rebukes the friends and restores Job double — vindication and renewed blessing.
Ps.1: Two ways: the righteous prosper like a planted tree; the wicked are chaff. Frames the wisdom-vs-folly arc of the whole Psalter.
Ps.2: The nations rage but God enthrones his Anointed Son. Royal-messianic psalm; quoted of Christ in Acts and Hebrews.
Ps.3: Davidic morning psalm fleeing Absalom — the LORD is a shield, sustainer of the lifted head.
Ps.4: Evening psalm of trust: 'In peace I will both lie down and sleep.'
Ps.5: Morning prayer for guidance against deceitful enemies; the righteous take refuge in God.
Ps.6: First penitential psalm — anguished plea for mercy; weeping turned to confidence God hears.
Ps.7: Plea for vindication against false accusation; God judges the righteous and tries hearts.
Ps.8: From the mouths of babes — the dignity of man crowned with glory, fulfilled in Christ (Heb 2). Cosmic praise of the divine name.
Ps.9: Thanksgiving for God's righteous judgment of the nations; the needy will not be forgotten.
Ps.10: Why does God stand far off? Lament over the wicked oppressor; God hears the desire of the afflicted.
Ps.11: Refuge in the LORD against counsel to flee; God tests the righteous and tests the wicked.
Ps.12: Prayer amid pervasive lying; the words of the LORD are pure silver, refined seven times.
Ps.13: How long, O LORD? Lament moving from forgottenness to renewed trust in steadfast love.
Ps.14: The fool says in his heart, 'There is no God' — universal corruption; cited in Romans 3.
Ps.15: Who may dwell on God's holy hill? Marks of the upright life that abides with God.
Ps.16: 'You will not abandon my soul to Sheol' — fulfilled in Christ's resurrection (Acts 2).
Ps.17: Davidic prayer of integrity; 'I shall be satisfied with your likeness when I awake.'
Ps.18: Royal thanksgiving for deliverance — the LORD as rock, fortress, and deliverer.
Ps.19: Twin revelation: the heavens declare God's glory and the law of the LORD is perfect, reviving the soul.
Ps.20: Royal prayer before battle: 'Some trust in chariots... but we trust in the name of the LORD our God.'
Ps.21: Thanksgiving for the king's victory granted by God's strength.
Ps.22: 'My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?' — Christ's cry from the cross; pierced hands and feet, then global praise.
Ps.23: The LORD is my shepherd — Davidic confidence in providence, presence, and provision through the valley to the table.
Ps.24: Who shall ascend the hill of the LORD? The King of glory enters his sanctuary.
Ps.25: Acrostic prayer for guidance and pardon — 'Remember not the sins of my youth.'
Ps.26: Plea for vindication based on integrity and love for God's house.
Ps.27: 'The LORD is my light and my salvation; whom shall I fear?' — confidence in God's presence in the temple amid foes.
Ps.28: Cry to the rock; thanksgiving when the LORD hears the supplication.
Ps.29: The voice of the LORD over the waters — sevenfold thunder of the storm-King enthroned forever.
Ps.30: Thanksgiving for healing: 'Weeping may tarry for the night, but joy comes with the morning.'
Ps.31: 'Into your hand I commit my spirit' — quoted by Christ on the cross.
Ps.32: Penitential psalm: blessedness of forgiven sin; confession brings relief, hiding it brings wasting. Quoted in Romans 4.
Ps.33: Praise the LORD with new song — by his word the heavens were made; the eye of the LORD on those who fear him.
Ps.34: 'Taste and see that the LORD is good' — acrostic of deliverance and the fear of the LORD.
Ps.35: Imprecation against false accusers; plea for divine warrior to contend.
Ps.36: Wickedness analyzed; God's steadfast love extends to the heavens — fountain of life.
Ps.37: Wisdom psalm: fret not because of evildoers; the meek shall inherit the land.
Ps.38: Penitential lament under God's chastening hand for sin; cry for nearness.
Ps.39: Brevity of life: 'Make me know my end' — silence before God amid sorrow.
Ps.40: 'I waited patiently for the LORD' — sacrifice not desired but a prepared body (Heb 10).
Ps.41: Blessed is he who considers the poor; betrayal foretold of Judas (Jn 13:18).
Ps.42: Thirsty soul: deer panting for water, hope amid downcast spirit. Pivotal psalm of Book II.
Ps.43: Companion to Ps 42 — 'Send out your light and your truth' to lead me to your altar.
Ps.44: Corporate lament: God seems to have rejected his people though they have not forgotten him.
Ps.45: Royal wedding psalm; the king addressed as God (Heb 1) — messianic bridegroom.
Ps.46: 'God is our refuge and strength... be still and know that I am God.' Inspired Luther's 'A Mighty Fortress.'
Ps.47: God reigns over the nations — clap your hands, all peoples!
Ps.48: Zion the city of the great King — beauty and security of God's dwelling.
Ps.49: Wisdom psalm on the futility of riches at death; God will ransom the soul from Sheol.
Ps.50: Asaph: God comes to judge his covenant people — true sacrifice is thanksgiving and obedience.
Ps.51: David's penitential cry after Bathsheba — 'Create in me a clean heart, O God' — the gospel of brokenness and cleansing.
Ps.52: Against the boastful tongue of Doeg the Edomite; the righteous flourish like an olive tree.
Ps.53: Variant of Ps 14 — the fool says there is no God; universal corruption.
Ps.54: Plea against the Ziphites; God is my helper.
Ps.55: Lament over a friend's betrayal: 'Cast your burden on the LORD.'
Ps.56: When seized by Philistines at Gath: 'In God I trust; I shall not be afraid.'
Ps.57: Hiding from Saul in the cave: 'My heart is steadfast, O God.'
Ps.58: Imprecation against unjust rulers; God judges on earth.
Ps.59: When Saul watched the house: 'O my Strength, I will sing praises to you.'
Ps.60: After military setback: God has rejected us — but with God we shall do valiantly.
Ps.61: 'Lead me to the rock that is higher than I' — refuge from the end of the earth.
Ps.62: 'For God alone my soul waits in silence' — exclusive trust in God as rock and salvation.
Ps.63: Davidic thirst in the wilderness: 'My soul thirsts for you... your steadfast love is better than life.'
Ps.64: Plea against secret schemers; God shoots them with arrows.
Ps.65: Harvest thanksgiving: God crowns the year with bounty.
Ps.66: Call all the earth to praise God for his works of redemption.
Ps.67: Missionary psalm: 'Let all the peoples praise you, O God' — Aaronic blessing extended to nations.
Ps.68: Triumphal march of God from Sinai to Zion; quoted in Eph 4 of Christ's ascension gifts.
Ps.69: 'Zeal for your house has consumed me' — passion psalm fulfilled in Christ; gall and vinegar.
Ps.70: Urgent plea: 'Make haste, O God, to deliver me!'
Ps.71: Prayer of an old man: 'O God, do not forsake me' — lifelong trust from youth to gray hairs.
Ps.72: Solomonic royal psalm — the messianic King's universal, righteous reign blessing the poor and all nations.
Ps.73: Asaph's crisis: envying the wicked until he entered the sanctuary and saw their end. 'Whom have I in heaven but you?'
Ps.74: Lament over the destruction of the sanctuary; appeal to God the Creator-King.
Ps.75: God alone judges; the cup of his wrath is poured out on the wicked.
Ps.76: God known in Judah — the warrior who breaks the weapons of war.
Ps.77: Asaph's distress turned to remembering the LORD's deeds at the Red Sea.
Ps.78: Historical psalm rehearsing Israel's rebellion and God's faithfulness from Egypt to David's choice.
Ps.79: Lament over the temple's defilement; plea for vengeance and restoration.
Ps.80: 'Restore us, O God; let your face shine, that we may be saved' — Shepherd of Israel implored.
Ps.81: Festival call to obedience: 'Open your mouth wide, and I will fill it.'
Ps.82: God judges among the gods/judges who fail the poor; cited by Jesus (Jn 10).
Ps.83: Plea against confederated enemies seeking to wipe out Israel.
Ps.84: 'How lovely is your dwelling place' — pilgrim's longing for the courts of the LORD.
Ps.85: Restoration prayer: 'Steadfast love and faithfulness meet; righteousness and peace kiss.'
Ps.86: Davidic prayer for an undivided heart to fear God's name.
Ps.87: Zion's glory: nations registered as born in her — anticipating the Gentile influx.
Ps.88: The darkest psalm — unrelieved lament: 'Darkness is my closest friend.'
Ps.89: Davidic covenant exalted then questioned amid apparent failure of the Davidic line.
Ps.90: Mosaic meditation on God's eternity and human frailty: 'Teach us to number our days.' Opens Book IV.
Ps.91: 'He who dwells in the shelter of the Most High' — sweeping promises of protection; quoted by Satan to Christ.
Ps.92: Sabbath psalm: the righteous flourish like a palm tree, the wicked perish.
Ps.93: The LORD reigns, robed in majesty — his throne established from of old.
Ps.94: God the avenger of evil; he disciplines but does not abandon his people.
Ps.95: Call to worship and warning not to harden hearts as at Meribah; expounded in Hebrews 3-4.
Ps.96: 'Sing to the LORD a new song' — proclaim his salvation among the nations; he comes to judge.
Ps.97: The LORD reigns; let the earth rejoice — clouds and thick darkness around him.
Ps.98: New song for new salvation; all creation joins the praise of the coming Judge.
Ps.99: The LORD reigns — holy is he! Threefold holiness and call to worship at his footstool.
Ps.100: 'Make a joyful noise unto the LORD' — universal call to enter his gates with thanksgiving; the Jubilate.
Ps.101: Royal pledge of integrity in court and household.
Ps.102: Penitential psalm of an afflicted man; God's eternity contrasted with man's fading days; cited in Heb 1 of Christ.
Ps.103: 'Bless the LORD, O my soul' — Davidic catalog of mercies; as far as east from west, so far has he removed our sins.
Ps.104: Creation hymn — God clothed with light, sustaining all creatures by his Spirit.
Ps.105: Historical psalm: God's faithfulness to the patriarchs and the exodus generation.
Ps.106: Companion to 105 — Israel's repeated rebellion and God's repeated mercy. Closes Book IV.
Ps.107: 'Let the redeemed of the LORD say so' — four pictures of distress and deliverance.
Ps.108: Composite of 57 and 60 — confidence and prayer for victory.
Ps.109: Imprecatory psalm against the betrayer; v.8 applied to Judas in Acts 1.
Ps.110: 'The LORD said to my Lord' — most-quoted OT text in NT; Christ as priest-king after Melchizedek.
Ps.111: Acrostic praising the LORD's works; 'the fear of the LORD is the beginning of wisdom.'
Ps.112: Acrostic on the blessedness of the God-fearing man — generous, steadfast, never moved.
Ps.113: First Hallel psalm — the LORD raises the poor from the dust to sit with princes.
Ps.114: Exodus song — sea fled, Jordan turned back, mountains skipped at God's presence.
Ps.115: Not to us, O LORD, but to your name be glory; idols mocked, the LORD trusted.
Ps.116: Personal thanksgiving for deliverance from death: 'I will lift up the cup of salvation.'
Ps.117: Briefest psalm — universal call to praise; cited by Paul (Rom 15:11) for Gentile inclusion.
Ps.118: Hallel climax: 'The stone the builders rejected has become the cornerstone' — sung at Passover, applied to Christ.
Ps.119: Massive acrostic celebration of God's word — 176 verses meditating on Torah as life, light, and delight.
Ps.120: First Song of Ascents — distress among Meshech and Kedar; longing for peace.
Ps.121: 'I lift up my eyes to the hills' — the keeper of Israel neither slumbers nor sleeps.
Ps.122: Joy at going up to Jerusalem: 'Pray for the peace of Jerusalem.'
Ps.123: Eyes lifted to God as servants to a master, awaiting mercy.
Ps.124: 'If it had not been the LORD who was on our side' — escape like a bird from the snare.
Ps.125: Those who trust in the LORD are like Mount Zion, immovable.
Ps.126: Returning from captivity: 'Those who sow in tears shall reap with shouts of joy.'
Ps.127: Solomonic — 'Unless the LORD builds the house...' Children a heritage from the LORD.
Ps.128: Blessed is everyone who fears the LORD — domestic flourishing.
Ps.129: Israel afflicted from youth, but the LORD has cut the cords of the wicked.
Ps.130: De profundis: 'Out of the depths I cry to you' — penitential plea grounded in plenteous redemption.
Ps.131: Davidic humility — soul calmed and quieted like a weaned child with its mother.
Ps.132: Davidic covenant remembered; God's promise of an enduring throne.
Ps.133: 'How good and pleasant when brothers dwell in unity' — like oil and dew.
Ps.134: Closing Song of Ascents — call to nighttime temple servants to bless the LORD.
Ps.135: Praise the LORD's name; he chose Jacob; idols are nothing.
Ps.136: The Great Hallel — 'his steadfast love endures forever' — antiphonal recital of creation and redemption.
Ps.137: By the rivers of Babylon — exile lament; how shall we sing the LORD's song in a foreign land?
Ps.138: Davidic thanksgiving: God regards the lowly; he will fulfill his purpose for me.
Ps.139: 'You have searched me and known me' — God's omniscience, omnipresence, and the wonder of fearful and wonderful design.
Ps.140: Plea for protection from violent men.
Ps.141: 'Set a guard, O LORD, over my mouth' — evening prayer like incense.
Ps.142: Maskil from the cave — pouring out complaint; 'You are my refuge.'
Ps.143: Penitential cry: 'Enter not into judgment with your servant, for no one living is righteous before you.'
Ps.144: Davidic praise of God as warrior trainer; prayer for national flourishing.
Ps.145: Acrostic of God's greatness — 'The LORD is good to all... his mercy is over all that he has made.'
Ps.146: Praise to the LORD who keeps faith forever — trust not in princes.
Ps.147: God heals the brokenhearted, gathers the outcasts, names the stars.
Ps.148: Cosmic hallelujah — sun, moon, sea creatures, and people summoned to praise.
Ps.149: New song of the saints — the high praises of God in their mouth and a two-edged sword in their hand.
Ps.150: Final crescendo — thirteenfold call to praise with every instrument: 'Let everything that has breath praise the LORD!'
Prov.1: Purpose of Proverbs: wisdom begins with the fear of the LORD; warning against sinners' enticements; Lady Wisdom's first cry.
Prov.2: The treasure-hunt for wisdom — diligent seeking yields the knowledge of God and protection from the wicked.
Prov.3: 'Trust in the LORD with all your heart' — wisdom's blessings; honor God with firstfruits.
Prov.4: A father's pleas to embrace wisdom; 'Above all else, guard your heart, for everything you do flows from it.'
Prov.5: Warning against the adulteress; rejoice in the wife of your youth.
Prov.6: Caution against suretyship, sloth, the seven things the LORD hates, and adultery.
Prov.7: The seduction of the simple young man by the adulteress — vivid drama of folly.
Prov.8: Lady Wisdom's grand speech — present at creation, possessed by the LORD before his works of old. Read of Christ by the church.
Prov.9: Wisdom and Folly each set a feast and call the simple — the climactic choice ending the prologue.
Prov.10: Begin Solomonic proverbs: contrasts between righteous and wicked, wise and foolish in tongue and labor.
Prov.11: Honest scales delight the LORD — proverbs on integrity, generosity, and the fall of the wicked.
Prov.12: The righteous love discipline; words have power to wound or heal.
Prov.13: A wise son hears instruction; hope deferred makes the heart sick.
Prov.14: Wisdom and folly contrasted — the path of the upright vs the way of the wicked; righteousness exalts a nation.
Prov.15: 'A soft answer turns away wrath' — the power of the tongue and the eyes of the LORD everywhere.
Prov.16: Sovereignty proverbs: 'The heart of man plans his way, but the LORD establishes his steps.'
Prov.17: A friend loves at all times; the crucible tests silver, the LORD tests hearts.
Prov.18: 'The name of the LORD is a strong tower'; an offended brother is harder to win than a fortress.
Prov.19: Better the poor of integrity than the rich liar; whoever is kind to the poor lends to the LORD.
Prov.20: Wine is a mocker; honest weights please the LORD; the king's heart is in his hand.
Prov.21: 'The king's heart is a stream of water in the hand of the LORD'; sacrifice without justice is abomination.
Prov.22: A good name is more desirable than great riches; train up a child in the way he should go. Begin sayings of the wise.
Prov.23: Don't toil to be rich; honor parents; the woes of drunkenness vividly depicted.
Prov.24: Wisdom for life and labor; do not rejoice when your enemy falls.
Prov.25: Hezekiah's collection of Solomon's proverbs — kingly wisdom; coals of fire for the enemy's head.
Prov.26: The fool, the sluggard, and the gossip — sharp portraits of social folly.
Prov.27: 'Iron sharpens iron'; faithful are the wounds of a friend; tend your flocks.
Prov.28: The wicked flee when none pursues; whoever conceals sins does not prosper.
Prov.29: Where there is no vision/prophecy the people cast off restraint; rod and reproof give wisdom.
Prov.30: Agur's confession of ignorance and the wonder of God's word; numerical sayings of wonder and folly.
Prov.31: The valiant woman of noble character — wisdom personified in the household, capping Proverbs' pursuit of practical godliness.
Eccl.1: 'Vanity of vanities' — the Preacher's thesis: weariness of life under the sun and the cyclical futility of all things.
Eccl.2: Solomon's experiments with pleasure, work, and wisdom — all hevel; yet enjoyment is from God's hand.
Eccl.3: 'A time for everything' — God has made everything beautiful in its time and set eternity in the human heart.
Eccl.4: Oppression, rivalry, and isolation; two are better than one; a threefold cord is not quickly broken.
Eccl.5: Reverence in worship — guard your steps in God's house; the love of money never satisfies.
Eccl.6: The vanity of unsatisfied wealth; better is the sight of the eyes than the wandering desire.
Eccl.7: Better the day of death than birth; the heart of the wise in the house of mourning; God made man upright but they sought schemes.
Eccl.8: Submission to authority and the mystery of God's timing; injustice under the sun.
Eccl.9: One event happens to all — death levels everything; whatever your hand finds to do, do with all your might.
Eccl.10: Wisdom in small matters — a little folly outweighs much wisdom; the order of a kingdom.
Eccl.11: 'Cast your bread upon the waters'; rejoice in youth, but know God will bring you into judgment.
Eccl.12: Conclusion: Remember your Creator before old age and death. Fear God and keep his commandments — the whole duty of man.
Song.1: The bride longs for her beloved's kisses — the opening duet of love's pursuit. Read of Christ and the church by the Reformed tradition.
Song.2: 'I am my beloved's and my beloved is mine' — spring awakening; the voice of the beloved at the lattice.
Song.3: The bride seeks her beloved by night and finds him; Solomon's wedding procession.
Song.4: The bridegroom's praise of the bride from head to foot — 'You are altogether beautiful, my love; there is no flaw in you.'
Song.5: The bride's lost opportunity and renewed search; she extols her beloved's surpassing beauty to the daughters of Jerusalem.
Song.6: The bride is found in the garden; she is unique among many — fair as the moon, bright as the sun.
Song.7: Mutual delight in the lover's beauty and the joy of consummated love.
Song.8: 'Love is strong as death' — the seal upon the heart; many waters cannot quench love. Climax of covenant love.
Isa.1: The great arraignment: Judah is a sick nation, but though sins be as scarlet they shall be as white as snow.
Isa.2: The mountain of the LORD's house exalted; nations stream to Zion, beat swords into plowshares; idols cast away.
Isa.3: Judgment on Jerusalem's leaders — props removed, women's finery stripped; sin invites societal collapse.
Isa.4: A remnant called holy under the Branch of the LORD; cloud and fire shelter restored Zion.
Isa.5: Song of the vineyard — God sought grapes but found wild ones; six woes and looming Assyrian darkness.
Isa.6: Isaiah's commissioning vision — holy, holy, holy; a sinner cleansed by altar coal sent to a hardened generation.
Isa.7: Ahaz refuses a sign; the LORD gives one anyway: Immanuel — virgin-born pledge of God-with-us.
Isa.8: Maher-shalal-hash-baz; fear God, not coalitions; to the law and testimony — bind up the testimony among disciples.
Isa.9: A great light dawns in Galilee — unto us a Child is born, Wonderful Counselor, Mighty God, Prince of Peace.
Isa.10: Assyria the rod of God's anger will itself be broken; a remnant of Jacob will return to the Mighty God.
Isa.11: The Branch from Jesse's stump rules in righteousness; wolf with lamb, knowledge of the LORD fills the earth.
Isa.12: A two-stanza song of salvation — the LORD is my strength and song; draw water with joy from salvation's wells.
Isa.13: Oracle against Babylon — the day of the LORD comes cruel; cosmic darkness on the proud city.
Isa.14: Taunt over Babylon's fallen king (typifying Satan's pride); promise of mercy to Jacob; Assyria broken.
Isa.15: Lament over Moab — sudden ruin in the night, weeping on every housetop.
Isa.16: Moab's pride brought low; a throne established in steadfast love — anticipates the Davidic Messiah.
Isa.17: Damascus reduced to a ruin; Israel's harvest stripped; men will look to their Maker, not idols.
Isa.18: Cush warned: God watches quietly from his dwelling, then prunes the nations like vine shoots.
Isa.19: Egypt judged then healed — an altar to the LORD in Egypt; Egypt, Assyria, Israel a threefold blessing.
Isa.20: Isaiah walks naked and barefoot three years — sign that Egypt and Cush will be led away by Assyria.
Isa.21: Fall of Babylon foreseen — 'fallen, fallen is Babylon'; oracles on Edom and Arabia.
Isa.22: Jerusalem rebuked for revelry on the eve of judgment; Shebna deposed, Eliakim given the key of David.
Isa.23: Tyre humbled — the trading city laid waste; her wealth one day consecrated to the LORD.
Isa.24: The Little Apocalypse — earth laid waste for breaking the everlasting covenant; the LORD reigns on Mount Zion.
Isa.25: Banquet on the mountain — God swallows up death forever and wipes away every tear.
Isa.26: Song of trust — you keep him in perfect peace whose mind is stayed on you; resurrection hope: the dead shall live.
Isa.27: Leviathan slain; the LORD's vineyard tended; Israel gathered one by one with the great trumpet.
Isa.28: Woe to drunkards of Ephraim; God lays in Zion a tested cornerstone — line upon line.
Isa.29: Ariel besieged; lip-service worship rebuked; the deaf shall hear and the meek find joy.
Isa.30: Woe to those running to Egypt; in returning and rest you shall be saved; the LORD waits to be gracious.
Isa.31: Egypt is flesh, not spirit; the LORD himself will defend Jerusalem like a hovering bird.
Isa.32: A king will reign in righteousness; until the Spirit is poured out from on high, and justice dwells in the wilderness.
Isa.33: Woe to the destroyer; the LORD is exalted; sinners in Zion afraid — who can dwell with consuming fire?
Isa.34: Day of the LORD's vengeance against Edom — heavens rolled up like a scroll.
Isa.35: The wilderness blooms; ransomed of the LORD return to Zion with everlasting joy on their heads.
Isa.36: Rabshakeh's blasphemous taunt at Jerusalem's wall — trust no god to deliver from Assyria's hand.
Isa.37: Hezekiah spreads the letter before the LORD; the Angel strikes 185,000 Assyrians in one night.
Isa.38: Hezekiah's illness, prayer, and fifteen added years; the sundial sign and his psalm of thanks.
Isa.39: Babylonian envoys flattered; Isaiah foretells exile — Hezekiah's foolish complacency.
Isa.40: Comfort, comfort my people — voice in the wilderness; God carries lambs; those who wait renew their strength.
Isa.41: Fear not, I am with you; I uphold you with my righteous right hand; idols are nothing.
Isa.42: Behold my Servant — gentle with the bruised reed, a covenant for the people, a light for the nations.
Isa.43: When you pass through waters I will be with you; you are mine; I, I am he who blots out your transgressions.
Isa.44: Spirit poured on offspring; idolatry mocked — the man warms himself with one half and worships the other.
Isa.45: Cyrus named God's anointed shepherd; turn to me and be saved, all the ends of the earth — to me every knee shall bow.
Isa.46: Bel and Nebo bow; God carries Israel from the womb to gray hairs — idols must be carried, the LORD carries.
Isa.47: Lament over virgin Babylon — her sorceries and pride cannot save; sudden widowhood and shame.
Isa.48: Stubborn Israel refined in the furnace of affliction; come out from Babylon — for my name's sake I defer my anger.
Isa.49: Second Servant Song — too small a thing to restore Jacob; I will make you a light to the nations.
Isa.50: Third Servant Song — the Servant gives his back to those who strike, sets his face like flint.
Isa.51: Look to the rock from which you were hewn; awake, awake, arm of the LORD; comfort for Zion.
Isa.52: Awake, Zion; how beautiful are the feet of him who brings good news; my Servant shall be high and lifted up.
Isa.53: The Suffering Servant — pierced for our transgressions, crushed for our iniquities. The clearest OT preview of substitutionary atonement.
Isa.54: Sing, barren one — your Maker is your husband; covenant of peace shall not be removed; no weapon shall prosper.
Isa.55: Come, everyone who thirsts — buy without money; seek the LORD while he may be found; his word shall not return empty.
Isa.56: Foreigners and eunuchs welcomed to the holy mountain; my house shall be a house of prayer for all nations.
Isa.57: Idolatrous adultery exposed; yet the LORD revives the spirit of the lowly; no peace for the wicked.
Isa.58: True fasting — loose chains of injustice, share bread; then your light shall break forth like the dawn.
Isa.59: Sin separates from God; no intercessor — so his own arm brought salvation; the Redeemer comes to Zion.
Isa.60: Arise, shine, your light has come; nations to your light; the LORD shall be your everlasting light.
Isa.61: The Spirit of the Lord GOD is upon me — to preach good news to the poor; the text Jesus opened in Nazareth.
Isa.62: Zion renamed Hephzibah and Beulah; watchmen on the walls give God no rest till Jerusalem is a praise.
Isa.63: The Warrior from Edom in blood-stained garments; remembrance of God's mercies in the wilderness.
Isa.64: Oh that you would rend the heavens and come down; we are the clay, you the potter; intercession in ruin.
Isa.65: I was found by those who did not seek me; new heavens and a new earth; Jerusalem a joy.
Isa.66: Heaven my throne, earth my footstool; the humble and contrite tremble at God's word; final judgment and consolation.
Jer.1: Jeremiah called from the womb; touched on the mouth; set over nations to pluck up and to plant.
Jer.2: Two evils: Israel forsook the spring of living water and hewed broken cisterns that hold none.
Jer.3: Faithless Israel called to return; backsliding children, return — I am your husband; promise of restored shepherds.
Jer.4: Disaster from the north; circumcise your hearts; Jeremiah's anguished vision of creation undone.
Jer.5: Search Jerusalem's streets — none does justice; prophets prophesy lies and people love it so.
Jer.6: The siege approaches; ask for the ancient paths; they have healed the wound lightly, crying peace, peace.
Jer.7: Temple sermon — do not trust deceptive words 'the temple of the LORD'; reform your ways or be cast out.
Jer.8: No balm in Gilead? My people refuse to know the time of the LORD's judgment; the harvest is past.
Jer.9: Oh that my head were waters; let him who boasts boast in this — that he understands and knows the LORD.
Jer.10: Idols are scarecrows in a cucumber field; the LORD is the true God, the living God, the everlasting King.
Jer.11: Covenant proclaimed; Anathoth plot to kill Jeremiah — like a gentle lamb led to slaughter, he commits his cause to God.
Jer.12: Jeremiah complains: why do the wicked prosper? God answers — if you have raced with men on foot, how with horses?
Jer.13: The ruined linen loincloth — Israel was meant to cling to God; can the leopard change its spots?
Jer.14: Drought and famine; false prophets condemned; Jeremiah pleads, but God says do not pray for this people.
Jer.15: Even Moses and Samuel could not move God; Jeremiah's lament and God's call to renewed ministry.
Jer.16: Jeremiah forbidden to marry as a sign of judgment; coming exodus greater than the first.
Jer.17: Sin engraved with iron stylus; cursed who trusts in man, blessed who trusts in the LORD; Sabbath call.
Jer.18: The potter and the clay — God reshapes nations; plot against Jeremiah; he prays for vindication.
Jer.19: Jeremiah smashes the clay flask in Hinnom — so will God shatter this people and city.
Jer.20: Pashhur strikes Jeremiah; the prophet's confession — fire shut up in my bones; cursed be the day I was born.
Jer.21: Zedekiah inquires; God says he himself fights against Jerusalem; choose life — surrender to Babylon.
Jer.22: Oracles against Judah's kings; Shallum, Jehoiakim with shameful burial; Coniah a signet ring cast off.
Jer.23: Woe to bad shepherds; the righteous Branch — the LORD our Righteousness; false prophets denounced.
Jer.24: Two baskets of figs — exiles to Babylon are the good figs; Zedekiah's remnant the bad.
Jer.25: Seventy years of Babylonian exile foretold; the cup of God's wrath passed to all nations.
Jer.26: Trial of Jeremiah for the temple sermon; Uriah martyred; Ahikam shields Jeremiah.
Jer.27: Jeremiah wears a yoke — submit to Nebuchadnezzar, my servant; do not listen to false prophets.
Jer.28: Hananiah breaks Jeremiah's wooden yoke and prophesies peace; God replaces it with iron — Hananiah dies.
Jer.29: Letter to the exiles — build houses, seek the city's peace; plans to give a future and a hope after seventy years.
Jer.30: Day of Jacob's trouble, but he shall be saved; David their king restored; their wound, though incurable, will be healed.
Jer.31: The new covenant promise: God will write his law on hearts, forgive sin, and remember it no more — fulfilled in Christ (Heb 8).
Jer.32: Jeremiah buys a field at Anathoth during the siege — prophetic act that houses, fields, and vineyards shall again be bought.
Jer.33: Call to me and I will answer; the Branch of David will execute justice; the covenant with day and night ensures David's line.
Jer.34: Zedekiah frees slaves then re-enslaves them — God will give them freedom to sword, plague, famine.
Jer.35: The Rechabites' faithfulness to their father shames Judah's faithlessness to her God.
Jer.36: Jehoiakim cuts and burns Jeremiah's scroll; God dictates it again with more added words.
Jer.37: Jeremiah imprisoned in a cistern dungeon; Zedekiah secretly inquires — the king of Babylon shall surely come.
Jer.38: Lowered into miry cistern; Ebed-Melech the Cushite lifts him out with rags; secret counsel to Zedekiah.
Jer.39: Jerusalem falls; Zedekiah's sons slain before him, then his eyes put out; Jeremiah set free.
Jer.40: Gedaliah governor of the remnant; warned of Ishmael's plot but disbelieves.
Jer.41: Ishmael murders Gedaliah and many pilgrims; Johanan rescues the captives.
Jer.42: The remnant asks Jeremiah to pray; God says stay in the land, fear not the king of Babylon — but they have decided otherwise.
Jer.43: Defying God they flee to Egypt, dragging Jeremiah; Nebuchadnezzar's throne foretold over Egypt.
Jer.44: The remnant in Egypt insists on burning incense to the queen of heaven — total judgment pronounced.
Jer.45: Personal word to Baruch — seek not great things for yourself; I will give you your life as a prize of war.
Jer.46: Oracle against Egypt — Pharaoh's army crushed at Carchemish; do not fear, O Jacob my servant.
Jer.47: Oracle against the Philistines — overflowing waters from the north sweep them away.
Jer.48: Oracle against Moab — humbled for his pride; yet I will restore the fortunes of Moab in latter days.
Jer.49: Oracles against Ammon, Edom, Damascus, Kedar, Elam — God scatters them to every wind.
Jer.50: Oracle against Babylon — fallen, fallen; Israel and Judah weep their way back to Zion seeking the LORD.
Jer.51: Babylon's full destruction; the LORD is the portion of Jacob; scroll cast into the Euphrates with stone bound.
Jer.52: Historical postscript — Jerusalem's fall, temple plundered; Jehoiachin released and given a seat above kings.
Lam.1: Jerusalem widowed and weeping; she has none to comfort her; her sins are her undoing.
Lam.2: The LORD has become an enemy — judgment on his sanctuary; cry out, pour out your heart like water.
Lam.3: I am the man who has seen affliction; yet his mercies are new every morning — great is your faithfulness.
Lam.4: Gold grown dim; siege horrors recounted; punishment of Zion accomplished; Edom warned.
Lam.5: Final prayer — restore us to yourself, O LORD, that we may be restored; renew our days as of old.
Ezek.1: Wheels within wheels; four living creatures; the likeness of the glory of the LORD by the Chebar canal.
Ezek.2: Son of man, stand on your feet; sent to a rebellious house; eat the scroll of lamentation.
Ezek.3: The scroll sweet as honey; appointed watchman — accountable for warning; opened mouth only when God speaks.
Ezek.4: Sign-acts: brick siege of Jerusalem, lying on his side 390 + 40 days, defiled bread.
Ezek.5: Ezekiel's hair shaved and divided — sword, fire, scattering for Jerusalem's worse-than-the-nations sin.
Ezek.6: Mountains of Israel hear the word — high places shattered; a remnant left to remember.
Ezek.7: The end has come — disaster after disaster; silver and gold cannot deliver in the day of wrath.
Ezek.8: Vision of temple abominations — image of jealousy, elders incensing creatures, women weeping for Tammuz, sun-worshipers.
Ezek.9: The man with the inkhorn marks the foreheads of those who sigh; six executioners begin at the sanctuary.
Ezek.10: Glory of the LORD departs the threshold; coals scattered; cherubim and wheels lift off.
Ezek.11: Judgment on Jerusalem's wicked counselors; promise of a new heart of flesh; glory leaves the city.
Ezek.12: Ezekiel digs through the wall — sign of the prince's exile; the vision is for now, not far off.
Ezek.13: Woe to the foolish prophets who whitewash flimsy walls and sew magic charms on wrists.
Ezek.14: Idols of the heart; even Noah, Daniel, Job could deliver only themselves; four severe judgments.
Ezek.15: Vine wood — useless for anything but fuel; Jerusalem's residents cast into the fire.
Ezek.16: Allegory of the foundling bride — adorned and unfaithful; yet God will remember and atone.
Ezek.17: Two eagles parable — Zedekiah's broken oath; God will plant a tender twig that becomes a noble cedar.
Ezek.18: The soul who sins shall die — not the father for the son; turn and live; God has no pleasure in death.
Ezek.19: Lament for Israel's princes — lion cubs caught and caged; the vine plucked up and planted in the desert.
Ezek.20: History of rebellion from Egypt; for my name's sake I acted; coming new exodus and purging.
Ezek.21: The sword of the LORD polished and sharpened; Nebuchadnezzar at the crossroads; crown removed.
Ezek.22: Bloody city of dross — princes, priests, prophets all corrupt; God sought a man to stand in the gap and found none.
Ezek.23: Oholah and Oholibah — Samaria and Jerusalem as faithless sisters who lust after foreign lovers.
Ezek.24: The boiling pot; Ezekiel's wife dies and he is forbidden to mourn — sign of the temple's fall.
Ezek.25: Oracles against Ammon, Moab, Edom, Philistia — they shall know that I am the LORD.
Ezek.26: Tyre's destruction foretold — scraped bare like a rock, her stones cast into the sea.
Ezek.27: Lament over Tyre the merchant ship — splendid in trade, shattered in the heart of the seas.
Ezek.28: The proud king of Tyre cast down; lament over the anointed cherub — Eden imagery many take of Satan.
Ezek.29: Pharaoh the great dragon in the Nile; Egypt a lowly kingdom never to rule again.
Ezek.30: Wail, the day of the LORD on Egypt; Nebuchadnezzar paid in Egypt for his Tyre labor.
Ezek.31: Egypt likened to a great cedar of Lebanon — felled and sent down to Sheol with the uncircumcised.
Ezek.32: Lament over Pharaoh — dragon dragged up; he and his hordes lie among the slain in the pit.
Ezek.33: Watchman renewed; news of Jerusalem's fall arrives; people hear but do not do.
Ezek.34: Woe to selfish shepherds; God himself will shepherd his flock and set one shepherd, my servant David, over them.
Ezek.35: Mount Seir judged — perpetual desolation for Edom's hatred against Jacob.
Ezek.36: Mountains of Israel restored; sprinkle clean water, give new heart and Spirit — for my name's sake.
Ezek.37: Valley of dry bones — resurrection of Israel by the Spirit's breath; promise of a re-united nation under the Davidic shepherd.
Ezek.38: Gog of Magog comes against restored Israel — the LORD will magnify himself in the eyes of many nations.
Ezek.39: Gog falls on the mountains of Israel; seven months of burial; God's name no longer profaned.
Ezek.40: Vision of the new temple begins — measuring reed, gates, courts; meticulous holiness.
Ezek.41: Inner temple measured — nave, Most Holy Place, side chambers, carved cherubim and palm trees.
Ezek.42: Priestly chambers measured; outer wall — separation between holy and common.
Ezek.43: Glory of the LORD returns to the temple from the east; altar specified; this is the place of my throne.
